topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile efficiency of a bureaucratic decree. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the headline swam into view: "Groundbreaking Insights on Urban Decay" – the article was mine. But the byline beneath it read: "By Julian Vance." Julian Vance. A name I vaguely recognized from a competitor’s portfolio, a name that suddenly felt like a cheap imitation slapped onto my hard work. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and slowly, deliberately, I began to type a reply. My fingers moved with a cold, precise rhythm, each keystroke laced with a brittle, cutting sarcasm I couldn't quite suppress. I wanted to shred the document, to burn the entire submission, but the sheer audacity of the deception held me rooted, forcing me to channel my rising irritation into meticulous, venomous prose.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead magnifying the stark white of the notification. It was a slap in the face, delivered with a veneer of professionalism. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I reread the submission guidelines, the submission confirmation, tracing every line as if searching for the flaw, the moment where the fraud was revealed. When I finally found the discrepancy—the signature, the subtle stylistic shift—a wave of profound distaste washed over me. I pushed my chair back so sharply it scraped across the polished floor. I didn't shout; shouting was messy. Instead, I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the call button, my expression set in an unyielding mask of cool indifference. I needed to address this with surgical precision, to dismantle this unauthorized appropriation piece by piece, making it clear that this slight would not go unanswered.

[story 3]

A slow, heavy silence settled over the room as I processed the information. It wasn't anger that flared first, but a deep, chilling sense of being profoundly undervalued. I felt a slow burn starting low in my chest, radiating outward like heat from a faulty wire. The article, the culmination of weeks of sleepless nights and rigorous research, was being presented as someone else's achievement. I picked up the printed proof, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy and insulting in my hands. My gaze swept over the borrowed prose, and a muscle ticked violently in my jaw. I didn't immediately reach for my keyboard. Instead, I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city sprawl, feeling a profound sense of separation from the person who had somehow managed to steal my voice. When I finally turned back, my movements were deliberate, each step weighted with a quiet, simmering resentment.
